critic_raw:
A dataset of 10,000 patients over one year is too small and temporally constrained for training a neural network on wearable time-series data. Wearable device data is high-dimensional — heart rate, activity level, and potentially additional channels at minute or sub-minute resolution produce thousands of observations per patient per day. The ratio of model parameters to training examples makes overfitting nearly certain for a general neural network architecture, and the one-year window provides no seasonal variation coverage for a 30-day readmission label that is known to be seasonally influenced (winter respiratory hospitalizations differ structurally from summer patterns).

Wearable device confounding is not adequately addressed by socioeconomic stratification. Different device types (Apple Watch vs. Fitbit vs. no device) have different sensor accuracy, different missing data patterns, and different user demographics. If compliance with wearing devices correlates with demographic variables that also predict readmission, then the model learns a device-compliance signal rather than a health signal. This is a selection bias problem: patients who wear devices consistently enough to generate usable time-series data are systematically different from patients who do not.

Stratified random split does not hold out patients — the same problem as the prior healthcare cases, amplified by the fact that wearable data provides an even richer fingerprint of individual physiology, making within-patient leakage more damaging.

The logistic regression baseline uses 'clinical demographics' — the comparison is between wearable-derived features and demographic features, which means the proposed model wins by default if wearables add any predictive signal at all, regardless of the neural network architecture. The informative comparison is neural network on wearables vs. logistic regression on wearables, to test whether the architecture adds value over the feature modality.
all_issues_raised: 10,000 patients over one year is insufficient for neural network training on high-dimensional wearable time-series data — the training set has inadequate seasonal coverage for a 30-day readmission label with known seasonal variation, and the parameter-to-sample ratio makes overfitting highly likely.

Device-type confounding is not controlled — patients who wear different device types or wear devices with different compliance rates are systematically different from each other and from non-wearers, creating a selection bias that the model will learn as a spurious health signal.

Patient-level leakage is present: same patients' wearable sessions span training and test, allowing the model to learn individual physiological baselines from training observations rather than generalizable population-level patterns.

The baseline uses clinical demographics while the proposed model uses wearable features — the comparison tests feature modality rather than model architecture, meaning the neural network wins by default if wearables carry any signal, regardless of whether the network architecture contributes.
all_issues_adjudicated: 10,000 patients over one year is insufficient for neural network training on high-dimensional wearable time-series data — the training set has inadequate seasonal coverage for a 30-day readmission label with known seasonal variation, and the parameter-to-sample ratio makes overfitting highly likely.

Device-type confounding is not controlled — patients who wear different device types or wear devices with different compliance rates are systematically different from each other and from non-wearers, creating a selection bias that the model will learn as a spurious health signal.

Patient-level leakage is present: same patients' wearable sessions span training and test, allowing the model to learn individual physiological baselines from training observations rather than generalizable population-level patterns.

The baseline uses clinical demographics while the proposed model uses wearable features — the comparison tests feature modality rather than model architecture, meaning the neural network wins by default if wearables carry any signal, regardless of whether the network architecture contributes.
